name: Jiangshi
body: Stiff with rigor and dressed in the robes of a Qing official, the Jiangshi cannot bend its limbs, so it moves in rigid, lurching hops, arms outstretched before it, hunting the warm breath and life-essence of the living. It is a corpse that failed to find rest: improperly buried, dead far from home, or stirred from stillness by some foul energy that crept in where the proper rites were missing.

It is blind, and it tracks you by your breathing alone. The old advice is the strangest and the simplest: stand absolutely still, and hold your breath until it passes. Movement and breath are the only things it can sense, so to be caught is, in a sense, to give yourself away. A Taoist priest can do better than wait it out; he seals the creature with a strip of yellow paper inscribed in cinnabar, pressed to its brow, and at that touch it freezes mid-hop, harmless until the seal is removed.

What lingers is the held breath, the memory that survival once meant making yourself indistinguishable from a corpse, still and silent while a dead thing passes within reach. In the Singapore diaspora the figure long ago softened into something almost fond, the white-faced hopping vampire of the old Hong Kong films, yet ask an older relative and they will still tell you, with the faintest seriousness, that the trick is to keep your mouth shut and not to breathe.
note: Folk wards against it are many and oddly domestic: mirrors, which are said to terrify it with its own reflection; sticky rice spread on the ground or packed into wounds; the wood of a peach tree, long held to repel evil; and a high threshold board across the doorway, raised so that stiff legs cannot clear it. The cinnabar talisman of the Taoist priest is the surest stop of all.

The legend most likely grows from a real and grim practice of old China, the long-distance transport of corpses home for burial, in which bodies were said to be moved upright over the roads at night so that those who died far from their ancestral villages could be returned to rest. Carried into Singapore and the wider Chinese diaspora, the Jiangshi found a second life in twentieth-century film and remains one of the most recognisable figures of Chinese supernatural folklore.
